content.problemStatement:
You are given a positive integer NNN.

Construct an array AAA of length NNN such that:


−300≤Ai≤300-300 \leq A_i \leq 300−300≤Ai​≤300
∣Ai+Ai+1+Ai+2+…+Aj∣|A_i + A_{i+1} + A_{i+2} + \ldots + A_j|∣Ai​+Ai+1​+Ai+2​+…+Aj​∣ is **not divisible** by (j−i+1)(j-i+1)(j−i+1) for all pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) (1≤i<j≤N)(1 \leq i \lt j \leq N)(1≤i<j≤N).

Note that ∣X∣|X|∣X∣ denotes the absolute value of XXX. For example ∣−2∣=2|-2|=2∣−2∣=2 and ∣5∣=5|5| = 5∣5∣=5.
If multiple such arrays exist, print any.

It is guaranteed that there exists at least one valid array satisfying the given constraints.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single line of input.

The only line of each test case contains a positive integer NNN — the length of array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers, denoting array AAA.

If multiple such arrays exist, print any.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 500) – the required length of the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (‑300 ≤ Ai ≤ 300) such that for every pair (i, j) with 1 ≤ i < j ≤ N the value |Ai + Ai+1 + … + Aj| is NOT divisible by (j‑i+1). Any valid array may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 the array is [1,2]; subarray (1,2) sum=3, 3 mod 2 = 1 ≠ 0. For N=3 the array is [1,2,1]; all subarrays of length ≥2 have sums 3 or 4, none divisible by their lengths. For N=4 the array is [1,2,1,2]; every subarray sum equals length plus the number of 2's, which is never a multiple of the length.

solution.approach:
Observe that the condition is required only for subarrays of length at least 2 (i < j).  If we build the array alternating 1 and 2, i.e. Ai = 1 for odd i and Ai = 2 for even i, then any contiguous subarray of length L contains k occurrences of the value 2 where 1 ≤ k ≤ L‑1 (because two equal numbers never appear consecutively).  The sum of such a subarray is:
    sum = (L‑k)·1 + k·2 = L + k.
  Hence sum mod L = k, which is never 0 because k is neither 0 nor L.  The absolute value does not change the remainder.  Therefore the required property holds for every subarray of length ≥2.  For N = 1 there are no pairs (i, j), so any number (e.g., 1) is acceptable.

Algorithm:
  for each test case read N
  for i from 1 to N:
    if i is odd output 1 else output 2
  print a newline.
The construction uses only numbers 1 and 2, both within the allowed range.

Correctness Proof Sketch:
  *Lemma 1*: In the alternating sequence, any contiguous subarray of length L ≥ 2 contains at least one 2 and at most L‑1 twos.
  *Proof*: Because the sequence is 1,2,1,2,… two equal numbers never appear consecutively, so a subarray of length ≥2 cannot consist solely of 1s nor solely of 2s.
  *Lemma 2*: For such a subarray, let k be the number of 2s. Its sum S = L + k.
  *Proof*: Each 1 contributes 1, each 2 contributes 2, so S = (L‑k)·1 + k·2 = L + k.
  *Lemma 3*: L does not divide S.
  *Proof*: S mod L = (L + k) mod L = k mod L. By Lemma 1, 1 ≤ k ≤ L‑1, therefore k mod L ≠ 0.
  *Theorem*: The produced array satisfies the problem condition.
  *Proof*: For any i < j, let L = j‑i+1. By Lemma 3, |sum| = S is not divisible by L. Hence the condition holds for all required subarrays.

Complexities: Time O(N) per test case, total O(∑N) ≤ O(5·10⁴). Space O(1) besides output.
